title: Rust Fundamentals
description: Learn Rust from ownership and borrowing through traits, enums, pattern matching, and Cargo.

modules.title: Ownership & Borrowing
modules.description: Rust's core memory model — ownership, borrowing, and lifetimes.

sections.title: Ownership Move
sections.explanation: Rust's ownership model means assigning `s1` to `s2` MOVES the data. `s1` is no longer valid.

sections.title: Borrowing
sections.explanation: `&s` creates an immutable borrow. The function reads but doesn't take ownership, so `s` is still valid.

sections.title: Mutable References
sections.body_markdown: How many mutable references to a variable can exist at the same time in Rust?
sections.explanation: Rust enforces at compile time: you can have either ONE mutable reference OR any number of immutable references, never both.

sections.title: Lifetimes
sections.body_markdown: What do lifetime annotations (e.g., `'a`) in Rust specify?

choices.A: How long a variable lives in memory at runtime
choices.B: The relationship between the lifetimes of references so the borrow checker can validate them
choices.C: The size of the variable in bytes
choices.D: The thread on which the variable is allocated

sections.explanation: Lifetime annotations don't change how long references live — they tell the compiler how reference lifetimes relate to each other.

modules.title: Enums & Pattern Matching
modules.description: Enums, Option, Result, and match expressions.

sections.title: Enum with Data

choices.B: The variant Circle with its associated data 5.0

sections.explanation: Rust enums can carry data. `Shape::Circle(5.0)` stores both the variant identifier and the f64 value.

sections.title: Option<T>
sections.explanation: `x` is `Some(5)`, so the `Some(n)` arm matches with n=5, printing 5*2 = 10.

sections.title: Result<T, E>
sections.body_markdown: What is the idiomatic way to propagate errors in Rust?

choices.B: The `?` operator after a Result-returning expression

sections.explanation: The `?` operator unwraps Ok values or returns Err early. It's the standard way to propagate errors.

sections.title: if let
sections.explanation: `if let` destructures the Option. Since val is Some, s binds to "hello" and it's printed.

modules.title: Traits & Cargo
modules.description: Traits, generics, and the Cargo build system.

sections.title: Trait Basics
sections.explanation: The `Greet` trait implementation for User formats the greeting with the user's name.

sections.title: Derive Macros
sections.code_snippet:
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]
struct Point { x: f64, y: f64 }

choices.B: Auto-implement trait methods based on the struct's fields

sections.explanation: `derive` auto-generates trait implementations. Debug enables `{:?}` printing, Clone enables `.clone()`, PartialEq enables `==`.

sections.title: Cargo Basics

choices.B: Compiles the project with optimizations enabled

sections.explanation: `cargo build --release` compiles with optimizations (opt-level 3), producing a faster binary in `target/release/`.

sections.title: Cargo.toml
sections.body_markdown: Where are Rust project dependencies declared?
sections.explanation: `Cargo.toml` is Rust's manifest file. Dependencies go under the `[dependencies]` section.
